<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: virtual-context-abi
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/virtual-context-abi.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/virtual-context-abi.qmd@feeac6c71
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Virtual Context ABI

Planning and compilation both need memory, but memory is dangerous when it
arrives as an anonymous pile of text. A model can be given a long prompt, a
retrieved passage, a summary, a private note, an old plan, or a source packet.
Those objects may all fit in the same context window, but they are not the same
kind of thing. They do not have the same authority, freshness, evidence value,
or permitted use.

The Virtual Context ABI is the interface that keeps those differences visible.
It sits between durable memory and the working context a model is allowed to
see. It says what can be addressed, which version is being mounted, which
snapshot is current, which representation is being used, what authority labels
travel with the packet, how long the packet is leased, and what kind of fault
must be reported if the context is missing, stale, unsafe, conflicting, or
inadequate.

The central reader idea is simple: context is not governed memory until it has
an address, a version, a lease, an authority boundary, and a fault path.

## Problem

Long-horizon agents need a stable interface between durable memory and
model-visible working context.

The ASI Stack cannot treat memory as "whatever is currently in the prompt." A
long-running system has old evidence, revised evidence, private material,
summaries, active plans, failed attempts, source notes, audit records,
permission constraints, and temporary task context. Some of that material
should be visible to a worker. Some should be visible only to a verifier. Some
should be visible only through a summary. Some should not be visible at all.

That is why VCM is framed as an ABI: a stable calling convention for context.
A planner should be able to request a context packet by semantic address,
version, mount, snapshot, representation need, authority label, adequacy target,
and lease boundary. The memory system should respond with a materialization
receipt or a typed fault, not a best-effort blob.

The distinction between durable memory and active context matters. Durable
memory is the store of things the system can potentially refer to. Active
context is the task-relative view the model is currently allowed to use. The
ABI governs the translation from one to the other.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Long context windows and ordinary retrieval do not define addressability,
evidence, authority, adequacy, or fault behavior.

A larger window gives the model more room. It does not tell the system whether
a passage is current, authorized, adequate for verification, safe for the
worker, or traceable to a source. Retrieval answers a different question:
"what looks relevant?" The ABI has to answer harder questions: which version,
under which mount, for which task, under which authority, with which
representation losses, and with what failure behavior?

The surrounding literature makes the pressure visible. RAG distinguishes
retrieved evidence from parametric memory. Long-context evaluations and
position-sensitivity work show that fitting text into a window is not the same
as reliably using it. Memory-operating-system approaches show that external
memory needs policy. Citation and self-retrieval work show why evidence must be
attached to generated claims. Prompt-compression work shows that representation
loss has to be named rather than hidden.

VCM does not claim superiority over those systems here. It turns their pressure
into a control interface: admitted context is not automatically adequate
context, and available memory is not automatically authorized context.

## Core Claim

Virtual Context Memory should expose a Virtual Context ABI with stable
addresses, versions, mounts, snapshots, fault classes, representation
contracts, and lifecycle operations. This remains an architectural argument.

The support is bounded. VCM public v1 supplies the control-plane vocabulary for
semantic objects, versions, representations, validations, packets,
materializations, authority binding, admission, adequacy, typed non-success,
and audit records. Context Engineer supplies the information-supply-chain and
need-to-know pressure. Verification Bandwidth explains why admitted context may
still be inadequate for checking a claim. VIEA connects context materialization
to the broader intent-to-execution spine. Editable VCM and MoECOT are useful
refinement and runtime-reference sources, but they do not provide local
resolver or runtime evidence.

The live chapter therefore stays at `argument` support. The repository has
schema validation, finite Lean hooks, and a synthetic admission/adequacy
harness. It does not have a deployed VCM resolver, planner-guided context
compiler, snapshot service, adequacy classifier, model-facing context run,
VCM-Bench reproduction, leak-prevention benchmark, or MoECOT runtime trace.

## Mechanism

The ABI turns a memory request into either a governed materialization or a
typed refusal.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Task context request"] --> B["Semantic address"]
  B --> C["Version and mount"]
  C --> D["Snapshot binding"]
  D --> E["Representation contract"]
  E --> F{"Admission allowed?"}
  F -- "no" --> G["Typed fault"]
  F -- "yes" --> H{"Adequate for target?"}
  H -- "yes" --> I["Leased materialization"]
  H -- "no" --> G
  I --> J["Context receipt"]
  G --> J
```

The diagram is not a resolver implementation. It shows the contract. A context
request must pass through address, version, mount, snapshot, representation,
admission, and adequacy before it becomes usable model-visible context. Failure
is not an exception to hide. It is one of the ABI's official outputs.

The mechanism separates four states that ordinary retrieval often collapses.
A request can be well formed. A reference can resolve. A packet can be
authorized. A materialization can be adequate for a particular target. Those
are not the same state. A packet may be admitted for drafting but inadequate
for verification. A source may be known but outside the worker's authority. A
summary may be useful for orientation but too lossy for citation. A snapshot
may be valid at one point and stale later.

The ABI should therefore emit receipts. A context receipt says what was
requested, what resolved, what representation was built, which authority labels
apply, what the packet may support, when the lease expires, and which fault or
residual remains. That receipt is what lets auditors reconstruct why a model
saw a packet and what the packet was allowed to mean.

## Interfaces

Planning requests context. VCM resolves and compiles it. Execution receives
only authority-appropriate materializations.

The minimum context ABI record includes request identity, task identity,
lifecycle state, request validity, semantic address, version, mount, mount
scope, snapshot, resolution validity, resolver policy, representation contract,
authority labels, authority ceiling, materialization validity, admission state,
adequacy state, adequacy requirement, fault state, materialization reference,
consumer policy, support boundary, lease expiry, replay boundary, source
references, residuals, audit references, support-state effect, and non-claims.

Those fields keep a simple phrase like "context available" from hiding too
much. Available for what? Under whose authority? From which snapshot? With
which representation loss? Adequate for drafting, verification, execution, or
only orientation? Reusable after the current task, or expired with the lease?

This is where VCM connects to the surrounding stack. Command contracts define
what the worker is allowed to do. Planning names the context need. Cognitive
Compilation says which semantic atoms require evidence. Semantic pages and
context cells carry smaller evidence boundaries inside the materialized packet.
Claim ledgers later decide what the packet can support.

## Invariants

Addresses and versions must be stable. A claim should not depend on "whatever
retrieval returned at the time." It should depend on a context reference that
can be reconstructed.

Admission and adequacy must remain distinct. A packet that is safe to show a
worker may still be inadequate for the claim being checked. A packet that is
adequate for orientation may be inadequate for proof, citation, release, or
self-improvement.

Faults must be typed and visible. Missing, stale, conflicting, revoked,
unauthorized, infeasible, unsafe, unknown, and inadequate context should not be
laundered into a confident answer. The correct output may be ask, abstain,
deny, retry, escalate, quarantine, or residualize.

The current Lean hooks are narrow but useful. They prove finite-record
properties: a resolved context reference must have a matching address, version,
and snapshot binding, and a mandatory context miss must produce a typed fault
instead of a best-effort materialization. They do not prove a real resolver or
context compiler.

## Failure Modes

Flat transcript memory loses addresses, versions, leases, authority labels, and
support boundaries. The model remembers text but not the contract around the
text.

Stale context makes an old correct answer look current. Without a snapshot and
lease boundary, the system may reuse a packet after its assumptions have
expired.

Silent unsafe fit is worse than refusal. A worker may receive context it should
not see, or use a packet for a purpose it was never authorized to support. That
failure is especially dangerous because it can look like helpful memory.

Fault compression is another failure. Several precise context faults can be
summarized into one vague note like "uncertain context." That destroys the
decision surface. A missing source, stale certificate, revoked mount,
unauthorized packet, and inadequate representation require different responses.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is a context ABI schema, valid and invalid
fixtures, finite proof hooks, and a synthetic harness that refuses to collapse
admission into adequacy.

The repository already has this narrow surface. `schemas/context_abi_record`
defines the public record shape. `AsiStackProofs.VirtualContextABI` covers the
finite snapshot-binding and mandatory-miss fault predicates. The context
admission/adequacy harness checks synthetic context ABI records, context
packets, semantic page certificates, context transactions, and adequacy records.
Its recorded local run passed three valid fixtures and rejected five
expected-invalid fixtures covering admission-as-verification, conflict
promotion, deleted derivative materialization, mode confusion, and stale
certificate use.

That is meaningful evidence for record discipline. It is not evidence for a
deployed VCM service. It does not prove semantic-address resolver correctness,
summary fidelity, contradiction-rate improvement, distractor resistance,
runtime memory-store behavior, or model-facing context quality.

The next practical slice should be a resolver fixture set: one valid request
that resolves to a leased materialization, one mandatory miss that emits a
typed fault, one resolved packet rejected as inadequate for verification, one
packet denied by mount policy, and one lease-expired reuse attempt blocked. It
would sharpen the ABI semantics without pretending to be a full memory product.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature Virtual Context ABI is a memory operating interface for long-horizon
AI work. It is not just a larger retrieval system. It is the stable syscall
layer that lets planners, compilers, workers, verifiers, and auditors request
context by address, version, mount, snapshot, representation need, authority
label, adequacy target, and lease boundary.

With that interface, a model never receives anonymous "relevant context." It
receives a materialization receipt. The receipt says what was requested, what
resolved, what representation was built, which authority labels apply, which
uses are permitted, when the lease expires, whether the packet is adequate for
the target claim, and which fault or residual remains. If any of those states
are missing, the system asks, abstains, retries, escalates, or records a typed
fault rather than hallucinating a usable memory view.

At product maturity, the context ABI would also support replay. A later audit
could reconstruct the exact context view used by a plan, job, claim, artifact,
or release. A downstream worker could request more authority or a richer
representation without inheriting ambient permission. A self-improvement
candidate could not smuggle stale or unauthorized context into its own
justification.

That endpoint is not implemented here. The book should not claim it until
resolver behavior, context-compiler traces, adequacy labels, conformance tests,
runtime audit records, and model-facing results are present.

## What Is Proven, And What Is Not

The current project checks schema shape, synthetic admission/adequacy
consistency, stale-certificate rejection, deletion-closure blocking,
mode-confusion blocking, and finite Lean lookup properties.

It does not prove VCM resolver behavior, context compiler behavior,
transactional memory-store correctness, summary fidelity, contradiction-rate
reduction, distractor resistance, leak prevention, deployed Digital SCIF
behavior, MoECOT runtime behavior, VCM-Bench performance, model-facing context
quality, or source-interpretation adequacy.

This distinction matters because memory systems are easy to overtrust. If a
packet feels relevant, the model may treat it as evidence. If a summary sounds
complete, the reader may treat it as faithful. If a context window is large,
the system may treat it as understanding. The ABI exists to stop those
collapses.

## Summary

The Virtual Context ABI makes context access explicit. It separates durable
memory from working context, gives context stable addresses and versions, binds
materializations to snapshots and leases, preserves authority labels, records
adequacy boundaries, and emits typed faults when memory cannot responsibly be
used.

The rule is not "retrieve more." The rule is "make memory accountable before it
shapes reasoning." A governed AI system should know what the model saw, why it
was allowed to see it, what it was allowed to infer, when the context expired,
and what failures remained.

The next chapter goes inside the packet. Semantic Pages, Context Cells, and
Certificates asks what each page, cell, summary, and compressed representation
must carry so evidence boundaries survive once context has been materialized.
